summary: The main launcher flow works, but there are still trust-level risks around teardown safety and QA target liveness that keep the runtime helper below a clean pass for unattended use.

finding_log.summary: `down` releases reserved ports even when process teardown reports failure, so a stuck runtime can keep listening on a port that the allocator immediately marks free for another ticket.
evidence: `stop_runtime_processes` records `running = not stop_process_group(pid)`, so a failed stop leaves `running: true` in process metadata.
`down_runtime_record` releases every reserved port unconditionally right after that metadata update, without checking whether any process is still marked running.
finding_log.next_action: Release ports only for services that actually stopped, or fail `down` when any tracked process remains running.

finding_log.summary: `qa` still advertises live frontend/backend targets after the runtime is stopped, so unattended QA can be directed to dead endpoints without any liveness signal.
evidence: After `status` reports `"status": "stopped"`, `python3 bin/ticket_runtime.py qa --ticket TASK-0099 --json` still returns `frontend_url` and `backend_url` under `open_targets`.
The helper currently never filters or annotates QA targets based on runtime status.
finding_log.next_action: Make `qa` include runtime status and either suppress `open_targets` or mark them unavailable when the runtime is stopped.

finding_log.summary: The proof covers the happy path, but it does not cover the exact failure-path behavior that matters for autonomous QA: partial teardown, stale QA targets, or failed restart replacement.
evidence: Tests prove start/stop success, compose command execution, and worktree guardrails, but there is no regression test for a stop failure or for `qa` after a stopped runtime.
If `up` stops an existing runtime and then fails before launching the replacement, the exception path does not persist the stopped-old-runtime metadata back to disk.
finding_log.next_action: Add regression coverage for teardown failure or simulated partial-stop behavior, for `qa` on stopped runtimes, and for failed-restart replacement state.

rubric_sections.name: code-quality
findings: The helper stayed reasonably compact and readable, but teardown semantics and QA liveness are still not modeled tightly enough for autonomous runtime management.
The most important missing piece is not more feature breadth; it is harder failure-path handling around stop/restart behavior.
rubric_sections.next_action: Tighten the stop/restart contract before expanding the helper further.

rubric_sections.name: integration-readiness
findings: The runtime helper can now start and stop processes, but teardown failure handling can still create port-collision risk.
QA routing is explicit, but not yet trustworthy enough for unattended use because stopped runtimes still look open to the `qa` surface.
rubric_sections.next_action: Fix teardown-port coupling and QA liveness reporting, then rerun review.

rubric_sections.name: evidence-quality
findings: The attached proof is strong for the happy path, but it does not yet defend the failure paths that matter for autonomous QA trust.
The latest review claims need more than a green smoke; they need regression proof for partial-stop and stopped-target behavior.
rubric_sections.next_action: Add failure-path evidence and rerun review.

blocking_findings: Ports are released even when a tracked runtime remains marked running after `down`.
`qa` advertises dead targets after the runtime has already been marked stopped.
next_action: Reopen TASK-0099, fix teardown safety and QA liveness semantics, add regression tests for those paths, and rerun review.
